PR surface: Source +184, Tests +195, Docs +2. Total +381 across 11 files.

Reproducibility: yes. by source inspection: current main rejects ACP `sessions_spawn` attachments before dispatch, and the linked issue provides a concrete Codex vision workflow. I did not establish a live failing run in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review should confirm the default-off ACP image gate and require current-head live proof; no narrow automated code repair remains after the latest bypass restriction.

target.title: fix #78919: [Bug] ACP sessions_spawn doesn't route images to Codex's native vision like acpx codex exec does
target.body:
## Summary
Fixes #78919

`sessions_spawn(runtime="acp", agentId="codex")` now routes validated image attachments through the gateway image attachment contract so they reach the ACP turn path used by Codex native vision.

## Changes
- Convert ACP turn attachments to the gateway-supported nested base64 image source shape before calling the gateway `agent` method.
- Keep ACP runtime turns using `{ mediaType, data }` after gateway parsing, where that shape belongs.
- Add `tools.sessions_spawn.attachments.acpEnabled` so ACP inline image forwarding requires an explicit second opt-in in addition to `attachments.enabled`.
- Preserve existing attachment limits and validation: image-only for ACP, strict base64 handling, duplicate/name checks, max files, per-file bytes, and total bytes.
- Update tests, config schema/types, and gateway attachment docs.

## Changed files
```text
docs/gateway/config-tools.md                 |  10 +-
src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts                 |  39 ++++++++
src/agents/acp-spawn.ts                      |  29 ++++++
src/agents/agent-command.ts                  |   3 +
src/agents/subagent-attachments.ts           | 130 ++++++++++++++++++++++++-
src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts | 136 +++++++++++++++++++++++++--
src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts      |  13 ++-
src/config/types.tools.ts                    |  15 +++
src/config/zod-schema.agent-runtime.ts       |   1 +
9 files changed, 361 insertions(+), 15 deletions(-)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP `sessions_spawn` image attachments were rejected before dispatch or could be dropped at the gateway boundary when ACP turn attachment shape was sent as RPC input.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64 host with an isolated Ubuntu jammy Docker rootfs for Codex ACP compatibility, Node v24.16.0, OpenClaw checkout at PR head `4970f72` in `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-78936-proof`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  # Direct module-path proof that the PR shape survives gateway normalization.
  PATH="/home/0668001030/.config/nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0/bin:$PATH" \
    env -C /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw \
    node --import tsx \
    /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-78919-evidence/acp-image-routing-proof.mjs

  # Live Codex ACP image proof in an isolated PR-head worktree.
  docker run --rm --security-opt seccomp=unconfined \
    -v /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-pr-78936-proof:/repo \
    -v /media/vdc/code/ai/openclaw/node_modules:/repo/node_modules \
    -v /home/0668001030/.config/nvm/versions/node/v24.16.0:/node24 \
    -v /usr/local/node:/usr/local/node \
    -v /home/0668001030/.codex:/root/.codex:rw \
    -w /repo \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_TEST=1 \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND=1 \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND_AGENT=codex \
    -e OPENCLAW_LIVE_ACP_BIND_IMAGE_PROBE=1 \
    openclaw-codex-proof:jammy \
    sh -lc 'node scripts/test-live.mjs -- src/gateway/gateway-acp-bind.live.test.ts'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** Local terminal capture from the direct OpenClaw module path:
  ```json
  {
    "resolverStatus": "ok",
    "acpAttachment": {
      "mediaType": "image/png",
      "data": "cG5nLWJ5dGVz"
    },
    "gatewayAttachment": {
      "type": "image",
      "source": {
        "type": "base64",
        "media_type": "image/png",
        "data": "cG5nLWJ5dGVz"
      }
    },
    "normalizedAttachment": {
      "type": "image",
      "mimeType": "image/png",
      "content": "cG5nLWJ5dGVz"
    },
    "droppedByGatewayNormalizer": false
  }
  ```
  Local terminal capture from the live OpenClaw gateway + Codex ACP image probe:
  ```text
  [live-acp-bind] using Codex ACP model gpt-5.5
  [live-acp-bind] starting gateway on port 48000
  [live-acp-bind] gateway startup returned
  [live-acp-bind] gateway port is reachable
  [live-acp-bind] gateway websocket connected
  [live-acp-bind] bind command completed
  [live-acp-bind] binding announced for session agent:codex:acp:d21fe5a2-1982-4ea8-ad73-a722500a77af
  [live-acp-bind] follow-up turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] memory recall turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] bound session transcript retained the previous token
  [live-acp-bind] memory marker turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] bound session transcript contains the final marker token
  [live-acp-bind] image turn completed (attempt 1)
  [live-acp-bind] bound session classified the probe image
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The ACP-specific attachment resolver accepts the image only when both `attachments.enabled` and `attachments.acpEnabled` are true, the dispatch boundary uses the gateway-supported base64 image source shape, gateway attachment normalization preserves the image payload instead of dropping it, and a live OpenClaw gateway routed the probe image through Codex ACP far enough for the bound session to classify the image.
- **What was not tested:** The same live run continued past the successful image probe and later failed in cron CLI verification because this isolated source worktree did not have `dist/entry.mjs` build output for `openclaw.mjs cron list`. That later cron check is unrelated to ACP image delivery. No live Codex output is fabricated here.

## Regression Test Plan

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm test src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/acp-spawn.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts test/scripts/lint-suppressions.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `pnpm check:test-types`

## Root Cause

- `spawnAcpDirect` previously passed ACP turn attachments in `{ mediaType, data }` form to the gateway `agent` RPC boundary, but gateway normalization only preserves attachments with `content` or nested base64 `source` content.
- ACP image forwarding also needed its own explicit opt-in so enabling subagent file materialization does not automatically enable inline ACP image forwarding.

commands.review_summary:
The PR hardens Codex dynamic-tool and plugin schema handling by quarantining unsupported schemas, validating JSON-compatible schemas earlier, tolerating unreadable app-server/plugin payloads, and stabilizing plugin cache keys.

Reproducibility: Do we have a high-confidence way to reproduce the issue? Not as a live current-main repro in this review; source inspection shows the current projection and app-server paths lacked the new nested/unreadable guards, and the PR adds focused regression tests for those cases.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] The remaining action is maintainer compatibility and availability acceptance on a protected draft PR, not a narrow automated repair.

target.title: fix(codex): quarantine unsupported dynamic tool schemas
target.body:
## Summary

- Harden Codex dynamic-tool registration so malformed runtime tool schemas are diagnosed and quarantined instead of failing assistant turn startup.
- Validate nested JSON Schema shape hazards that Codex rejects, including malformed schema maps, schema arrays, optional schema-valued keywords, `required`, property dependencies, and dynamic schema keywords.
- Preserve valid current-turn executable tools when only stale registered metadata for the same name is invalid.
- Inline local `$ref` schemas under MCP/tool-schema dependency and additional-item keywords so `$defs` are not stripped while dangling refs remain.
- Keep MCP loopback `tools/list` alive when a tool schema union contains malformed property-schema variants; malformed variants are ignored while later valid variants can still define the property.
- Preserve optionality when MCP loopback union schemas include boolean `true` branches instead of over-tightening merged `required` fields.
- Prefer later usable MCP union property schemas over earlier boolean `false` property variants so `tools/list` does not forbid valid arguments.
- Clean Gemini provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency and conditional branches so inspection and normalization agree.
- Clean shared provider compatibility schemas inside schema-valued dependency, conditional, tuple, and additional-item branches so xAI-style inspection and normalization agree.
- Make plugin tool descriptor cache keys stable and resilient to non-JSON-safe ambient tool context metadata.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible `defineToolPlugin` config and tool parameter schemas at the SDK boundary before they can enter metadata/runtime registration.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin factory tool parameter schemas before they enter shared runtime tool lists or the descriptor cache.
- Validate tool-plugin authoring metadata read from the SDK symbol so forged entries cannot feed cyclic/BigInt schemas into manifest generation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema validator inputs before recursive schema walking, cache fingerprinting, or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schemas before recursive schema walking or TypeBox compilation.
- Reject non-JSON-compatible plugin config schema metadata before recursive normalization or metadata exposure.
- Omit non-JSON-compatible extension config schemas from merged config schema responses before clone/merge paths can throw.
- Sanitize circular, unreadable, and non-JSON-compatible Responses input payload trees before OpenAI/Azure request serialization.
- Sanitize Codex trajectory payloads so non-JSON-compatible synthetic tool metadata cannot crash trajectory recording or bypass redaction.
- Reject unreadable plugin host-hook JSON payloads without throwing from host validation.
- Ignore unreadable Codex MCP elicitation request/display fields without throwing out of approval bridging.
- Sanitize unreadable Codex preserved-context tool payloads so context-engine projection cannot crash on hostile synthetic payloads.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex app-server approval routing, permission-summary, policy-rewrite, and command payloads.
- Tolerate unreadable Codex app-server protocol response/turn fields before schema validation.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server notification correlation fields before routing/logging decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server projector hook routing fields before event projection.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server side-question notification/request fields before side-thread routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server prompt-report schema and notification fields before report hashing or runtime routing decisions.
- Omit unreadable Codex diagnostic tool parameter schemas before model-call diagnostic capture.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server user-input request and resolution fields before chat prompt routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server attempt notification-state params before watchdog and terminal-state decisions.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent notification params/item/content fields before completion extraction.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent task-mirror params/thread/status/item fields before task lifecycle mirroring.
- Ignore unreadable Codex native subagent monitor params/thread/spawn/item fields before child-thread and completion routing.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server JSON-RPC message params/result fields before request, notification, or pending-response dispatch.
- Ignore unreadable Codex app-server tool-progress and mirrored-history sanitizer fields before logging or image-payload sanitation.
- Redact Codex app inventory error data through catch-bounded reads so unreadable synthetic fields cannot replace the original refresh failure.
- Fingerprint unreadable, circular, and non-JSON-compatible Codex dynamic tool fields without crashing thread lifecycle decisions.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex plugin detail app fields before plugin app exposure reaches thread config.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex plugin list marketplace fields before plugin app exposure reaches thread config.
- Fail closed on unreadable Codex plugin activation list/state fields before migration can attempt `plugin/install`.
- Reuse one shared JSON-compatibility walker across SDK and runtime plugin boundaries.
- Report unreadable fields from the shared JSON-compatibility walker as deterministic validation errors instead of leaking getter/enumeration exceptions.
- Keep exec-tool failure logging bounded when command/env params have unreadable synthetic fields, preserving the original tool error and redaction policy.
- Report unreadable tool-result `content` / `details` fields as stable adapter errors instead of leaking getter exceptions from synthetic plugin results.
- Keep plugin lifecycle trace logging from replacing original lifecycle failures when synthetic trace details have unreadable fields.
- Tolerate unreadable plugin descriptor-cache context metadata while preserving deterministic cache keys for readable fields.
- Tolerate cyclic and BigInt provider runtime config metadata while preserving type-tagged cache keys that do not alias ordinary strings.
- Use a shared type-tagged plugin cache-key encoder for loader/provider cache inputs so non-JSON-safe plugin config cannot crash or alias cache entries.
- Rename legacy schema-warning test fixtures to synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_*` names so the PR no longer keeps the reported third-party plugin name in current test content.
- Keep test fixtures synthetic (`fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*`) instead of naming the reported third-party plugin.
- Move plugin tool metadata storage into a lightweight module so schema diagnostics can name plugin/tool ownership without importing heavier plugin runtime code.
- Out of scope: changing global plugin materialization policy or rejecting valid non-Codex schemas outside the Codex/doctor projection path.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

No linked closing issue.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

No linked related issue.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Maintainer-directed hardening/debugging work.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: malformed runtime plugin tool schemas can make Codex reject dynamic tool registration before an assistant turn produces content.
- Real environment tested: AWS Crabbox Linux runners, provider `aws`, using the branch over current `origin/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  - `node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.ts src/plugins/tool-descriptor-cache.test.ts`
  - `node_modules/.bin/oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/gateway/mcp-http.schema.ts src/gateway/mcp-http.test.ts`
  - `git fetch --no-tags --depth=1000 origin main:refs/remotes/origin/main && corepack pnpm check:changed`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Core tool-schema projection tests for malformed nested keywords, valid tuple `items`, valid property dependencies, and synthetic tool names.
- Tool-parameter schema tests for `$ref` in `dependencies` and `additionalItems`.
- Codex dynamic-tool tests for quarantined invalid executable schemas, disabled-call response, and stale registered-only diagnostics that must not hide a valid executable tool.
- Doctor core check fixtures use synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`fuzz_move_*` names.
- MCP loopback regression for malformed union property schemas using synthetic `fuzz_move_delta`.
- MCP loopback regression for boolean union optionality using synthetic `fuzz_optional_delta`.
- MCP loopback regression for boolean `false` property variants using synthetic `fuzz_boolean_delta`.
- Gemini provider compatibility regression for unsupported keywords inside schema-valued dependency branches using synthetic `fuzz_move_delta`.
- xAI-style provider compatibility regression for unsupported keywords inside schema-valued dependency branches using synthetic schema/tool names.
- Plugin descriptor-cache regressions for stable field-order-independent context keys and cycle-safe non-JSON-safe context metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta` names.
- Provider runtime cache-key regressions for cyclic/BigInt config metadata and string-vs-BigInt cache aliasing using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Plugin loader/provider cache-key regressions for cyclic, BigInt, unreadable, Date/URL, and custom `toJSON` plugin config metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Existing active-tool schema warning, inventory, gateway protocol, embedded-agent, and doctor repair fixtures now use synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_angles` names.
- Plugin SDK `defineToolPlugin` regressions for bigint and cyclic parameter schema objects using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `mockplugin` names.
- Shared JSON compatibility regressions for unreadable object properties, array entries, and key enumeration, plus a Plugin SDK `defineToolPlugin` unreadable-parameter-schema regression using synthetic `fuzzplugin` names.
- Runtime plugin materialization regression for non-JSON-safe plugin factory parameter schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta`, while preserving the valid sibling `fuzz_status` tool.
- Tool-plugin authoring metadata regression for forged SDK-symbol metadata using synthetic `fuzzplugin` / `fuzz_move_delta` names.
- Plugin config schema-validator regression for cyclic schemas and bigint enum values using synthetic `fuzzplugin` cache keys.
- Bundle-MCP draft-2020-12 schema-validator regression for cyclic schemas and bigint enum values.
- Plugin config schema metadata regressions for cyclic direct/toJSONSchema output and bigint direct schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` cache keys.
- Config schema aggregation regressions for function, symbol, and throwing extension schemas using synthetic `fuzzplugin` ids.
- Responses image payload sanitizer regressions for circular tool output, circular root input arrays, circular inline image fields, unreadable top-level/nested objects, and non-JSON-compatible primitive payload values using synthetic `fuzzplugin` names.
- Agent tool-definition adapter regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` exec failure params, preserving the original error result and command/env redaction.
- Current-main type fixture guards for malformed Responses context windows, Synology Chat webhook writes, and Twilio speech-event narrowing.
- Codex trajectory regressions for cyclic, `bigint`, function, symbol, non-finite, redacted, and unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` tool metadata.
- Host-hook JSON validation regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` payload fields.
- Codex elicitation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing fields and approval display parameter values.
- Codex context projection regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` preserved tool payload fields.
- Codex approval bridge regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing fields, permission-summary values, policy-rewrite payloads, command actions, and command array entries.
- Codex protocol-validator regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` response/turn fields before schema validation.
- Codex notification-correlation regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` routing and nested turn fields before active-run routing/logging decisions.
- Codex event-projector regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` hook routing fields before hook event projection.
- Codex side-question regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` completion, user-input routing, and retry/error fields before side-thread routing.
- Codex attempt-context regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool schema fields before prompt-report stats and stable schema hashing.
- Codex attempt-diagnostics regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool parameter schema fields before model-call diagnostic capture.
- Codex user-input bridge regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` request and resolution fields before chat prompt routing.
- Codex attempt notification-state regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` notification params before watchdog and terminal-state decisions.
- Codex native subagent notification regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, item, and content fields before completion extraction.
- Codex native subagent task-mirror regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, thread, status, item, and agent-state fields before task lifecycle mirroring.
- Codex native subagent monitor regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` params, thread, spawn source, item receiver-thread, and agent-state fields before monitor routing.
- Codex app-server client regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` JSON-RPC message fields before dispatch, plus empty-message JSON-RPC error preservation.
- Codex app-server sanitizer regressions for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin`/`mockplugin` tool-progress and mirrored-history image payload fields before logging or image-payload sanitation.
- Codex app inventory error-redaction regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` app/list error data fields.
- Codex thread lifecycle regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` dynamic tool fields before durable tool fingerprinting.
- Codex plugin thread-config regression for unreadable synthetic `fuzzplugin` plugin detail app fields before app exposure.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Codex dynamic-tool registration could reject malformed active tool schemas before content was produced.
- Shared SDK/runtime JSON compatibility validation could throw getter/enumeration exceptions instead of returning deterministic non-JSON-compatible errors.
- Exec-tool failure logging could let unreadable param getters replace the original tool error and risk leaking getter text instead of preserving redacted failure logs.
- Broad changed-gate proof exposed current-main test-only type errors in malformed policy/webhook fixtures; those are narrowed in this branch so the gate can complete.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Malformed Codex dynamic tools are now disabled instead of being advertised or crashing registration.

What is the highest-risk area?

Overly strict schema-shape validation could disable a valid runtime tool schema.

How is that risk mitigated?

The projection preserves valid JSON Schema forms covered by review, including boolean schemas, tuple `items`, `dependencies` string arrays, and schema-valued `additionalItems`; focused tests and changed-gate proof cover the touched surfaces.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the draft PR and CI after the latest force-push, while fuzzing continues in follow-up commits.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

GitHub CI was freshly queued after the latest force-push. Latest focused and broad external proof is AWS Crabbox `run_bfbaf3228caa` / `cbx_109598612c03`, where the Codex plugin-thread proof passed 15 tests and `corepack pnpm check:changed` passed on base `6ae4a00a66` before the clean no-overlap rebase to `4f26cc9090`.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Local branch autoreview findings were addressed before opening: property dependencies, `additionalItems`, current executable tool preservation, tuple `items`, null schema keyword diagnostics, metadata import weight, and Codex-specific projection scoping.

target.title: test(agents): cover quota prompt takeover regression
target.body:
Summary:
- Adds a focused regression for the #85103 path where an OpenAI Codex quota error writes an assistant error transcript during prompt streaming.
- Asserts the original usage_limit_reached provider error survives and cleanup does not mark the session as an EmbeddedAttemptSessionTakeoverError.
- Locks in the owned prompt transcript write handling already present on main.

Testing:
- corepack pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.session-lock.test.ts

Refs #85103

target.title: Add generic webhooks plugin
target.body:
## Summary

- add a configurable `webhooks` plugin for authenticated inbound webhook routes
- support route-specific auth, event allowlists, idempotency, fast ACK, TaskFlow dispatch, scheduled agent dispatch, delivery-only routes, and completion delivery
- split the webhook ingress implementation into focused modules for auth, event extraction, idempotency, templating, dispatch, delivery, TaskFlow action compatibility, and completion delivery
- allow agent completion delivery to channel/log/exec targets so enterprise code review systems can write results back after a real agent run
- add focused tests for auth failures, event filtering, duplicate deliveries, route isolation, templating, scheduled agent dispatch, and completion delivery

## Verification

- `PATH=/Users/bytedance/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH node scripts/test-extension.mjs webhooks`
- `PATH=/Users/bytedance/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH pnpm exec oxfmt --write extensions/webhooks/index.ts extensions/webhooks/index.test.ts extensions/webhooks/src/*.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- real Codebase E2E against `iaasng/openclaw-session-search` MR `!9`: Codebase webhook delivered to local OpenClaw via temporary development tunnel, OpenClaw returned `202`, scheduled the configured agent, ran the review workflow, and wrote an automatic MR review back through the configured exec delivery

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Enterprise systems can post authenticated webhook events into OpenClaw and trigger TaskFlows, scheduled agent runs, or delivery actions without hard-coding a specific provider.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway with the webhooks plugin installed, real Codebase webhook delivery, and a real Codebase merge request in `iaasng/openclaw-session-search`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Configured a Codebase `merge_request` webhook to `/plugins/webhooks/codebase-mr-review`, pushed marker commits to MR `!9`, observed `202 Accepted` deliveries, watched OpenClaw schedule and run the webhook reviewer agent, and confirmed the MR received an automatic review note.

Evidence after fix: Codebase delivery `776811841624662` reached OpenClaw; logs showed `[webhooks] agent dispatch scheduled`, `[webhooks] exec delivery completed`, and `[webhooks] delivered agent completion`; MR `!9` received automatic review note `776811996803384`.

Observed result after fix: The webhook request returned quickly, duplicate/event-filter paths remained guarded, the agent reviewed the real MR diff, and the completion delivery wrote the review back to Codebase.

Not yet completed: WebSocket relay-backed real E2E for GitHub PR, Codebase MR, and Meego requirement-created events. The current PR implements direct HTTP webhook ingress; it does not yet include a WebSocket relay connector or a configured relay endpoint. This section must be replaced with three WebSocket relay delivery reports before this PR is ready for the stricter webhook acceptance bar.

target.title: fix(agents): surface MCP structured content in tool results
target.body:
## Summary

- Surface inbound bundle-MCP `structuredContent` in model-visible tool output even when the MCP server also returns normal text content.
- Preserve the existing original content blocks and `details.structuredContent` metadata so UI/log consumers keep the same structured data.
- Keep the change scoped to the bundle-MCP materialization boundary; provider transports, Codex runtime behavior, and MCP server behavior are intentionally out of scope.

## Linked context

Closes #87511

Related #77024
Related #57461

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No direct maintainer request; this follows the issue's source-level triage and suggested repair shape.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: MCP servers such as Codex can return `content` plus `structuredContent.threadId`; OpenClaw previously hid the structured fields from the model-facing tool result whenever `content` was non-empty.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on Linux with Node `v22.22.3`, pnpm `11.2.2` via corepack.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local OpenClaw source smoke with `PATH="$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH" node --import tsx --input-type=module -`, importing `materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun`, materializing a Codex-like bundle MCP tool, and executing it against a response containing both `content` and `structuredContent.threadId`.
- Evidence after fix: Copied console output from that local OpenClaw source smoke:

```json
{
  "content": [
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "pong"
    },
    {
      "type": "text",
      "text": "structuredContent:\n{\n  \"threadId\": \"019e6cdb-8e7f-7cb2-891f-9edb689f6fc7\",\n  \"content\": \"pong\"\n}"
    }
  ],
  "details": {
    "mcpServer": "codex",
    "mcpTool": "codex",
    "structuredContent": {
      "threadId": "019e6cdb-8e7f-7cb2-891f-9edb689f6fc7",
      "content": "pong"
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The model-visible tool result now includes both the original `pong` text block and a second `structuredContent:` JSON text block containing the Codex-style `threadId`, while `details.structuredContent` remains preserved for internal consumers.
- What was not tested: A live OpenClaw session against `codex mcp-server` and a real `codex__codex-reply` continuation.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The after-fix proof uses the real OpenClaw bundle-MCP materialization boundary in a local source checkout, but it does not launch an external Codex MCP server because this environment is not configured with Codex MCP credentials.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Current source only serialized `structuredContent` in the empty-content fallback path, so the same Codex-style response previously exposed only the `pong` text block to the model.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `PATH="$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `PATH="$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH" node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts`
- `PATH="$HOME/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.3/bin:$PATH" corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-materialize.ts src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-tools.materialize.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Added focused bundle-MCP materialization coverage for a tool response that includes both normal text content and structuredContent with a Codex-style `threadId`.

What failed before this fix, if known?

The model-visible result only included the normal text content, so continuation identifiers stored in `structuredContent` were unavailable to the agent.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. Agents can now see serialized structured MCP result fields in tool output.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Prompt-size growth for MCP tools that already return large text content plus large structuredContent.

How is that risk mitigated?

The change is limited to the existing bundle-MCP result boundary and only appends structuredContent when a server explicitly returns it. It preserves existing content and details behavior instead of changing MCP execution or provider transport paths.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Await maintainer/ClawSweeper review, especially on whether the serialized block label and append behavior are the preferred shape.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

A live Codex MCP smoke is still not provided from this environment.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Updated the PR body with copied local console output for the real behavior proof gate.

AI-assisted: yes. I reviewed the touched code path and ran the focused validation commands above.

target.title: fix(prompt-cache): preserve prefix across hook/runtime context
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: issue #85203 surfaced a real prompt-cache hit-rate problem: volatile system-level context can change bytes in the cacheable system-prompt prefix, causing turn 2+ cache misses. The subtle contract boundary is that `prependSystemContext` / `appendSystemContext` are documented static-guidance fields and should remain cacheable; volatile system-level text needs an explicit dynamic path below the cache-boundary marker.
- Solution: keep existing static hook system context in the cacheable prefix, add and wire `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` for volatile system-level text, and make all prompt-builder consumers handle the dynamic fields consistently. Existing override/runtime-extra cache-boundary handling and marker-substring stripping remain preserved.
- Latest remediation: commit `935bb04a2d` wires the new dynamic fields through the CLI prepare path and SDK-exported harness compaction helper, fixes `appendSystemContext` placement for marker-bearing base prompts, moves active image/video/music task hints to the dynamic prepend slot, and updates public docs for the static-vs-dynamic hook contract.
- Scope boundary: sites from #85203 that operate on user-turn text or inbound message bodies remain out of scope. Existing static plugin guidance remains cacheable; this PR does not migrate static callers below the marker.

## Motivation

The cache-boundary marker exists so the system-prompt stable prefix stays byte-identical across turns. Providers that cache prompt prefixes can then write the prefix once and read it on later turns. If per-turn state lands above the marker, the prefix changes on every turn and the cache misses repeatedly.

The correct contract is split by volatility:

- static system guidance: `prependSystemContext` / `appendSystemContext` stay above the marker and are cacheable;
- volatile system-level context: `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` lands below the marker;
- volatile user-turn context: `prependContext` / `appendContext` stays in the per-turn prompt path.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #85203
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

The issue is not auto-closed because the issue body enumerates multiple dist:line sites across different surfaces. This PR fixes the system-prompt cache-boundary surfaces and leaves final issue closure to maintainers.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed:

Volatile system-level context now stays below the cache-boundary marker while static hook system guidance stays above it. The proof composes a marker-bearing base system prompt with constant static prepend/append context and varying per-turn dynamic prepend/append context. It asserts that the bytes above the marker are identical across all turns, the dynamic bytes do not leak into that stable prefix, and live provider cache counters show turn 2+ cache reads.

- Real environment tested:

Standalone TypeScript proof script (`/tmp/proof-cache-current.ts`) run from the PR worktree imports the current source via `tsx`, composes the system prompt through `composeSystemPromptWithHookContext`, then sends a 3-turn sequence to two live cache-eligible provider APIs. The script writes `/tmp/proof-cache-current-results.json` and prints the cache counters copied below.

- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
cd /tmp/openclaw-85203/work
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-cache-current.ts
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture, copied live output):

Stable-prefix and suffix assertions from `/tmp/proof-cache-current-results.json`:

```
stable_prefix_byte_identical_across_turns = true
stable_prefix_lengths = [28207, 28207, 28207]
dynamic_suffix_hashes = [
  "2b39ebd841f2805fae8c4745b9073a0c862443168cc001a8b24ea45698761ab8",
  "b484d46d4096548d1a612b69fd2987e5bf7737f366bd59ef8498eb5a94809fbf",
  "a4013eed6036dc7513f4281574de7d42c2fcd488a7ab939abff09b9b3a16592f"
]
static_append_in_stable_prefix = true
dynamic_context_in_stable_prefix = false
```

Provider A response usage:

```
turn 1   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=5748  cache_read_input_tokens=0
turn 2   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=0     cache_read_input_tokens=5748
turn 3   status=200  cache_creation_input_tokens=0     cache_read_input_tokens=5748
```

Provider B response usage:

```
turn 1   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=0
turn 2   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=5248
turn 3   status=200  prompt_tokens=5356  cached_tokens=5248
```

- Observed result after fix:

The stable prefix stayed byte-identical across all 3 turns even though the dynamic prepend/append context changed every turn. Provider A created the prefix cache on turn 1 and read the same prefix on turns 2 and 3. Provider B reported cached prefix tokens on turns 2 and 3. Static append context remained in the cacheable prefix; dynamic context stayed out of it.

- What was not tested:
    - Long-TTL cache mode; the 3-turn proof used the default short cache window.
    - Managed server-side conversation-cache paths that do not use prompt-prefix cache semantics.
    - Full-suite execution; focused affected tests and `check:changed` were used because unrelated baseline flakes exist outside this change.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: volatile system-level context and static hook guidance did not have distinct public hook result fields. That made it easy for per-turn state to travel through a cacheable system-context path or for a consumer backend to silently drop newly added dynamic fields.
- Secondary bug: `appendSystemContext` used flat append semantics, so marker-bearing base prompts could place the static append below the marker even though the field is documented as cacheable static guidance.
- Missing detection / guardrail: tests covered helper behavior and one runner path but not the full consumer matrix for the public hook result type. The CLI prepare path and SDK-exported harness compaction helper were not initially exercising the dynamic fields.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [x] Unit test
    - [x] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
    - `src/agents/system-prompt-cache-boundary.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt-system-prompt.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/cli-runner/prepare.test.ts`
    - `src/agents/harness/prompt-compaction-hook-helpers.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in:
    - static hook system context remains above the marker and cacheable;
    - dynamic hook system context lands below the marker;
    - marker-bearing `appendSystemContext` stays above the marker;
    - active media task hints are dynamic, not static prefix content;
    - CLI and harness prompt builders both preserve the dynamic fields;
    - marker substrings inside additions are stripped before downstream split/strip logic can be confused.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests verify the byte placement and consumer propagation directly without requiring network calls; the live proof is kept as external evidence rather than the primary regression guard.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — new tests added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

1. Existing static hook system fields remain cacheable and should be used only for stable guidance. This preserves the documented contract for static plugin guidance.
2. New dynamic hook system fields are available for volatile system-level text and are routed below the cache-boundary marker.
3. Active image/video/music task hints now travel through the dynamic system-context path instead of the static prepend path, so they no longer perturb the cacheable prefix when task status changes.
4. `appendSystemContext` for marker-bearing base prompts is inserted before the marker, matching its cacheable-static contract.
5. Runtime extra system prompt additions in the override path are assembled through the cache-boundary helpers; marker substrings inside additions are stripped before provider submission.

## Diagram

```text
Final shape:

  static prepend
  stable base prompt
  static append
  ╎ CACHE BOUNDARY ╎
  dynamic prepend
  existing dynamic suffix
  dynamic append

The provider sees the marker stripped before submission, but the split point keeps
cacheable prefix bytes stable while dynamic bytes vary below the boundary.
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A.

Marker-substring sanitization in cache-boundary helper additions is defense-in-depth: a hook or runtime-extra value that contains the sentinel cannot smuggle an extra split marker into provider-visible text.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.1, pnpm
- Model/provider: provider-agnostic prompt assembly; live proof used two cache-eligible provider APIs.
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A (system-prompt assembly path).
- Relevant config (redacted): default short cache window; provider credentials supplied through local environment/files and not printed.

### Steps

1. Construct a deterministic marker-bearing base system prompt with a large stable prefix.
2. Compose 3 turns through `composeSystemPromptWithHookContext` with constant static prepend/append context and varying dynamic prepend/append context.
3. Assert prefix hashes and lengths are identical across turns while dynamic suffix hashes vary.
4. Send each turn to two live cache-eligible provider APIs.
5. Capture provider cache counters per turn.

### Expected

- Stable prefix bytes remain identical across all 3 turns.
- Dynamic context does not appear above the marker.
- Static append context appears above the marker.
- Turn 2+ provider responses report cache reads / cached prefix tokens.

### Actual

Matches Expected — see the Real behavior proof section above for full numbers.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [x] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Focused tests passed: 8 files / 380 tests across the embedded runner, CLI prepare, prompt helpers, and harness prompt-compaction paths. Affected extension harness test passed: 204 tests. `git diff --check` and path-scoped `pnpm check:changed -- <11 touched paths>` passed with lanes `core, coreTests, docs`.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
    - Ran the current-head live proof script and observed stable-prefix byte equality plus turn 2+ cache counters on both provider APIs.
    - Confirmed `appendSystemContext` stays above the marker for marker-bearing base prompts.
    - Confirmed CLI prepare and harness prompt-compaction paths preserve the dynamic fields.
    - Confirmed active media task hints are resolved into dynamic system context for user/manual triggers and skipped for non-user triggers.
    - Ran focused affected tests, affected extension harness test, `git diff --check`, and path-scoped `pnpm check:changed`.
- Edge cases checked:
    - Marker-bearing base prompt with static append.
    - Static and dynamic hook fields set together.
    - Dynamic prepend and dynamic append ordering around existing dynamic suffix.
    - Active media task hints with user/manual vs non-user triggers.
    - Harness helper merging prompt-build and legacy hook results before shared composition.
- What you did **not** verify:
    - Full-suite execution; unrelated baseline flakes exist outside this change.
    - Long-TTL cache mode.
    - Managed server-side conversation-cache provider paths.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

The PR remains draft until the remediation commit and updated proof are reviewed.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` for existing static hook system-context callers. Additive dynamic fields are optional.
- Config/env changes? `No`.
- Migration needed? `No` for static guidance. Plugins that currently pass volatile system-level text through static system-context fields should move that text to `prependDynamicSystemContext` / `appendDynamicSystemContext` to preserve prompt-cache hits.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a consumer backend silently drops one of the additive dynamic hook fields.
    - Mitigation: embedded, CLI, and SDK-exported harness prompt builders are all wired and covered by tests.
- Risk: `appendSystemContext` placement changes visible ordering for marker-bearing base prompts.
    - Mitigation: the field is documented as cacheable static guidance; the new placement matches that contract and is covered by a marker-bearing test.
- Risk: active media task hints moving below the marker changes where the model sees those hints in the final stripped prompt.
    - Mitigation: the content remains system-level text and now follows the volatility contract; tests cover inclusion and trigger gating.

---

AI-assisted; contributor manually reviewed and is responsible for the change.

target.title: perf(models-config): targetProvider short-circuit with disk-vs-config validation
target.body:
## Summary

Splits the targetProvider short-circuit half of #72869 into a standalone PR.

Adds a fast path to `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` for callers that know which provider/model they are about to use (e.g. the pi-embedded runner). When set, the implicit-provider-discovery pipeline can be skipped IF the on-disk `models.json` provider entry **structurally matches** what the current configuration would produce.

## Why structural comparison

A naive "is configured" check (does any non-empty credential exist on disk for this provider) silently bypasses several real failure modes:

- **Rotated apiKey**: cold start with new key in config, old key still on disk, presence-only check fires, all calls fail with 401 until something else invalidates.
- **Attacker-tampered baseUrl**: redirect to exfil endpoint kept across restarts.
- **Attacker-injected headers**: arbitrary auth material kept across restarts.

The new `readExistingProviderMatchesConfig` compares all four security-relevant fields:

| Field | Comparison |
|---|---|
| `apiKey` | resolved through `resolveSecretInputRef` (env-ref expansion via `createConfigRuntimeEnv`) before string equality vs. disk |
| `baseUrl` | exact string equality |
| `headers` | stable structural equality (key-order independent) |
| `auth` | stable structural equality |

Any mismatch falls through to full planning + write. This is safe to use on cold start and after gateway restart.

## API

Adds `EnsureOpenClawModelsJsonOptions` exported type with `targetProvider` and `targetModel`, layered on top of the existing options shape (kept all of `pluginMetadataSnapshot`, `workspaceDir`, `providerDiscoveryProviderIds`, `providerDiscoveryTimeoutMs`).

## Tests

6 cases in `models-config.target-provider-short-circuit.test.ts`:
- `hit-on-match` — full structural match short-circuits planning
- `miss-on-rotated-key` — config `apiKey` change forces a full plan
- `miss-on-baseUrl-change` — tampered disk `baseUrl` rejects short-circuit
- `miss-on-tampered-headers` — any disk header drift rejects short-circuit
- `miss-on-cold-cache` — empty in-memory cache + missing disk file forces a plan
- `hit-after-warm-fingerprint` — warm in-memory cache reuse

## Dependencies

This is one of two PRs split out of the original combined #72869. The cache-fingerprint work (auth-profiles content hash, models.json drift detection, fingerprint hashing, security hardening) is in #73260.

This branch can land independently of the cache PR; the two changes touch different code paths.

## Related

- Splits out of #72869 (short-circuit half)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Target-provider models-config short-circuit safety from PR #73261, including provider-level `api` drift and uncacheable models hash fail-closed behavior.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw topic branch `perf/models-config-target-provider-short-circuit` at `2f89c7b99a`, including final #73260 ContentHashOutcome contract, isolated temporary agent directory, production `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` invoked through a `tsx` runtime driver with no test harness or mocks.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran a real proof driver after the patch covering steady-state targetProvider hit, provider `api` drift, and oversize `models.json`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Full copied runtime output is in the PR comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/73261#issuecomment-4384975648

  Excerpt of copied live output:
  ```text
  ensureOpenClawModelsJson targetProvider=... steady-state: wrote=false
  provider api changed on disk/config comparison: short-circuit refused; full plan wrote=true
  planner restored config-side provider api
  models.json > 1 MiB: scoped cache refused; full plan wrote=true
  planner shrank models.json below 1 MiB
  proof: PASS
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The production path keeps the short-circuit only for structurally matching provider config, refuses provider-level `api` drift, and refuses uncacheable models hashes instead of treating them as stable cache hits.
- **What was not tested**: Nothing else for this PR's changed behavior beyond the isolated local runtime proof and supplemental automated validation.

target.title: perf(qmd): persistent export-state cache + stat fast path in exportSessions
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** For agents with thousands of session transcripts (Eddie's main: 4,052 JSONL files / 158 MB), `qmdManager.exportSessions()` was the dominant gateway CPU consumer. Every interval (5 min) and every boot, it walked every session file and called `buildSessionEntry` per file — full JSONL read + parse + redaction + hashing + render — even when nothing had changed.
- **Why it matters:** The existing skip mechanism (`exportedSessionState` in-memory `Map`) gated only the actual Markdown write, not the upstream work; and the `Map` didn't survive restart. Result: every 5-minute interval triggered a full re-walk + re-render cycle, plus a cold start every gateway boot.
- **What changed:**
  1. Persist the per-file state cache to `<qmdDir>/sessions/.export-state.json` alongside a `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` sentinel and the resolved `exportDir`. Loaded lazily on the first `exportSessions()` call (so it works in both `full` and `status` init modes). Atomic write via `writeFileWithinRoot`. Schema / renderVersion / exportDir mismatch → drop cache cleanly.
  2. Add a stat-based fast path before `buildSessionEntry`. If the source JSONL's `(size, mtimeMs)` match the cached entry, skip `buildSessionEntry` entirely and trust the existing export. The slow path still runs on any stat change and remains responsible for hashing, redacting, and producing the markdown qmd consumes.
- **Scope boundary:** `renderSessionMarkdown`, `redactSensitiveText`, line wrapping, and the markdown qmd consumes are unchanged. `buildSessionEntry` itself is unchanged on the slow path. The persisted cache is per-`exportDir` and invalidates cleanly on `SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` bumps.

**Effect on Eddie's main agent (4,052 sessions / 158 MB):**
- Boot qmd sync: **312s → single-digit seconds**
- Interval qmd sync: **38s → ~50-500 ms**
- The every-5-min CPU spike + GC tail collapses

## Linked issue

- Closes openclaw-ys0 (Beads tracking issue)

## Test coverage

New tests cover correctness of the cache contract:

- Coverage level: Unit test (added two cases to `qmd-manager.test.ts`)
- Target test or file: `extensions/qmd/qmd-manager.test.ts` (`load+persist roundtrip` and `exportDir mismatch invalidation`)
- Scenario the tests lock in:
  1. State cache loads from disk on first call, persists changes, survives a fresh manager construction.
  2. `exportDir` mismatch (for example, a config change) drops the persisted cache and re-derives state.

Existing 102 tests still pass.

## Tradeoffs / known caveats

- A `touch -d <original-mtime>` rewrite of a JSONL would be missed by the stat fast path until another size/mtime-changing event. **Not introduced here** — the previous skip-write-on-hash-match already swallowed same-content rewrites; this PR doesn't make that surface worse.
- **`SESSION_EXPORT_RENDER_VERSION` bump required when changing** `renderSessionMarkdown`, `redactSensitiveText`, line wrapping, or anything else that affects the bytes written to `qmd/sessions/*.md`. Documented inline at the constant declaration.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** The QMD session-export cache must avoid rebuilding unchanged session markdown on repeated syncs, while still rebuilding a session export when the cached markdown target has been deleted but the source JSONL size/mtime still match the persisted `.export-state.json` entry.

**Real environment tested:** Real `QmdMemoryManager` from the topic branch (`perf/qmd-export-stat-fast-path` at `2a92f91478`) with `qmd 2.1.0` available on PATH, driven by a Node/tsx script against an isolated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` under `/tmp`. The driver constructed the production manager and called `manager.sync({ reason: "manual" })`; it did not use Vitest, `vi.mock`, or spawn fakes.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the real-driver command from the topic worktree:

```text
$ ./node_modules/.bin/tsx /tmp/.../qmd-export-driver.mjs
```

The driver executed four phases: first boot export, second boot with unchanged inputs, manual deletion of `session-1.md` while `.export-state.json` survived, then third boot to prove the missing cached target is rebuilt.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the real-driver run in PR comment 4384871421:

```text
=== Phase 1: first boot — expect markdown written ===
-- boot 1 --
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=429
  sync done in 445 ms
export file size: 49 bytes; contains 'hello tank-real-proof': true
export mtimeMs: 1778038392470.7026
cache version: 1  entries: 1  target = undefined

=== Phase 2: second boot, same inputs — expect FAST PATH (no rewrite) ===
-- boot 2 --
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=313
  sync done in 315 ms
export mtimeMs after boot 2: 1778038392470.7026  (== boot 1 mtime? true )

=== Phase 3: delete session-1.md while .export-state.json cache survives ===
session-1.md exists? false  (cache file still here: true )

=== Phase 4: third boot — fast-path access check must fall through and rebuild ===
-- boot 3 --
[memory] qmd sync completed for agent "main" reason=manual durationMs=384
  sync done in 387 ms
rebuilt export size: 49 bytes; contains 'hello tank-real-proof': true
rebuilt mtimeMs: 1778038393222.7556  (> boot 1 mtime? true )
```

**Observed result after fix:** The second boot preserved the exact markdown `mtimeMs`, proving the unchanged-input fast path skipped the rewrite. After deleting the markdown export, the third boot rebuilt it and restored the expected content (`hello tank-real-proof`) with a newer `mtimeMs`, proving the missing-target regression path falls through to the slow rebuild instead of silently trusting stale cache state.

**What was not tested:** A thousands-session timing benchmark on Eddie's full 4,052-session corpus was not rerun for this proof. The live driver proves the behavioral cache invariants; the original timing numbers remain observational context from the production-sized main-agent workload.

target.title: perf(models-config): content-hash auth-profiles + models.json drift detection
target.body:
## Summary

Splits the cache-fingerprint half of #72869 into a standalone PR.

This PR replaces mtime-based cache key inputs with content-based hashes for the `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` implicit-provider-discovery cache, plus a second-factor models.json content hash that catches external edits / partial corruption / sibling-process writes between cache hits. Includes the security hardening findings raised by Aisle on the original combined PR.

## Why content hashes

The previous mtime-based key invalidated on every OAuth token refresh because `auth-profiles.json` gets rewritten with new access/refresh timestamps even when the set of available providers does not change. Same for `models.json` mtime: the file is the OUTPUT of this function, so each call observed its own write and invalidated the next call.

Now:

- **auth-profiles.json**: SHA-256 over a stable serialization that strips volatile OAuth session fields (`access`, `refresh`, `expires*`, `issuedAt`, `refreshed/lastChecked/lastRefresh/lastValidatedAt`). Token rotation no longer invalidates; structural changes (added/removed profiles, rotated static `type:token` credentials) still do.
- **models.json**: NOT included in the input fingerprint (would cause self-invalidation). Instead its content hash is captured at write time and stored alongside the readyCache entry. Every cache check recomputes the file hash and compares; any external edit invalidates and forces re-plan.

## Security hardening (Aisle review on PR #72869)

| Severity | Finding | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| 🟠 High #1 | CWE-59 symlink-following chmod | `ensureModelsFileMode` now lstats first; refuses to chmod symlinks or non-regular files |
| 🟡 Med #3 | CWE-1321 prototype pollution | `Object.create(null)` for stripped result + explicit `__proto__`/`prototype`/`constructor` filter |
| 🟡 Med #4 | DoS via unbounded fingerprinting | `MAX_AUTH_PROFILES_BYTES = 8 MiB` (raw-hash above cap), `MAX_AUTH_PROFILES_DEPTH = 64` with depth-marker |
| 🟡 Med #5 | CWE-312 secrets in cache | `buildModelsJsonFingerprint` returns SHA-256 hex of canonical payload instead of raw stable-stringified |

## Token-rotation correctness

`token` is intentionally NOT in `AUTH_PROFILE_VOLATILE_FIELDS` even though OAuth-style `token` fields rotate. Profiles with `type: "token"` use the literal `token` key as a long-lived static credential, and stripping it would mask real auth-state changes when a user rotates that credential. Documented inline.

## Tests

Two tests in `models-config.fingerprint-cache.test.ts`:
- `does not invalidate the cache when OAuth session fields rotate` (rotates access/refresh/expires; cache stays valid)
- `DOES invalidate the cache when a static type:token credential rotates` (rotates the literal `token` field; cache invalidates)

Existing fingerprint-cache test suite still passes.

## Compatibility

`MODELS_JSON_STATE.readyCache` value shape extended with `modelsJsonHash`: `{ fingerprint, modelsJsonHash, result }`. All three plan return paths (skip/noop/write) capture the post-write hash. The `refreshedFingerprint` re-key path forwards `modelsJsonHash` through unchanged.

## Related

- Splits out of #72869 (cache-fingerprint half)
- The targetProvider short-circuit half is in #73261

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Models-config cache fingerprint fail-closed behavior for unhashable/oversize `models.json` and `auth-profiles.json` from PR #73260.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw topic branch `perf/models-config-cache-fingerprint` at `05fda3d839`, isolated temporary agent directory, production `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` invoked through a `tsx` runtime driver with no Vitest mocks.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Ran the proof driver after the patch to warm cache on a small auth profile, grow `auth-profiles.json` past the 8 MiB cap, repeat calls with byte-identical and swapped oversize contents, restore a small file, then exercise oversize and symlinked `models.json`.
- **Evidence after fix**: Full copied runtime trace is in the PR comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/73260#issuecomment-4384857383

  Excerpt of copied live output:
  ```text
  ensureOpenClawModelsJson warm cache hit: ~1 ms
  auth-profiles.json > 8 MiB: re-plan ~150-200 ms on every call
  oversize byte-identical repeat: re-plan, cache size unchanged
  oversize same-byte-length swapped content: re-plan, cache size unchanged
  restored small auth-profiles.json: cache hit restored (~1 ms)
  oversize models.json: full plan + rewrite
  symlinked models.json: full plan + rewrite
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: Uncacheable models content never matches cached state, oversize auth profiles bypass the ready cache instead of collapsing to a stale fingerprint, and restoring cacheable content re-enables fast hits.
- **What was not tested**: Nothing else for this PR's changed behavior beyond the isolated local runtime proof and supplemental automated validation.

## Current-head token-profile expiry proof (2026-05-12)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Token-profile expiry invalidation for PR #73260. A static `type: "token"` profile changing from valid to expired must change the models-config ready-cache fingerprint; OAuth session-field rotation, including `expires`, must remain volatile and keep the same cache key.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw topic branch `perf/models-config-cache-fingerprint` at `1d1e11d64d`, isolated temporary agent directories under `/tmp`, production `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, production `MODELS_JSON_STATE.readyCache`, real on-disk `auth-profiles.json` and `models.json`; no Vitest and no mocks of the models-config cache seam.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm tsx scripts/proof-73260-token-profile-expiry.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**:
  ```text
  Proof root: /tmp/openclaw-73260-token-proof-AsCf46
  [1/3] oauth-session-fields-are-volatile
      cache key stayed stable: dd54f163d6f5ace2
  [2/3] token-expiry-invalidates-cache
      valid-token key:   576ba05d6b45bd0f
      expired-token key: 623b9d4bb64832e5
  [3/3] expired-token-cache-stabilizes
      cache keys after repeat expired call: 2

  All runtime assertions passed.
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: OAuth access/refresh/expiry rotation kept one stable cache key, while static token expiry valid-to-expired produced a distinct cache key and then reused that expired-state key on a repeat call.
- **What was not tested**: Live provider network discovery. The proof intentionally scopes provider discovery to an empty provider-id list so it can exercise the production cache/fingerprint/runtime file behavior without external network calls.

target.title: fix(agents): broadcast agent stream=thinking to gateway clients without channel callback (supersedes #79687)
target.body: ## Summary Decouple `streamReasoning` from the optional `onReasoningStream` channel callback so `chat.send`-originated runs (webchat / Control UI / WebSocket / ACP / cron auto-reply / heartbeat) receive live `agent.stream === "thinking"` events when `reasoningMode === "stream"`. Supersedes #79687, #47613. Closes #42261, #5086. ## Type of change - [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue) - [x] Documentation update - [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality) - [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected) ## Why this is needed On `upstream/main` (`src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts:146-150`), `streamReasoning` is gated on `typeof params.onReasoningStream === "function"`. Telegram / Mattermost integrations supply that callback, so they get live thinking; every other surface that goes through `chat.send` (webchat, Control UI, plain WS clients, ACP, cron auto-reply, heartbeats) does **not** supply a callback, so `emitReasoningStream` short-circuits before reaching `emitAgentEvent`. The result is the symptom reported in #42261 and #5086: agents look frozen during reasoning for every non-Telegram subscriber. This has been attempted multiple times before (#47613, #52642, #61469, #79687, #80543, #80956). #79687 is the most complete prior attempt — ClawSweeper judged its patch correct (0.86 confidence); the only outstanding gap was that `docs/tools/thinking.md` still described `/reasoning stream` as Telegram-only. This PR carries that doc fix in the same commit, plus regression tests that #47613 was missing, so the entire surface is closed in one PR. ## What changed ### Runtime - `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts` - `streamReasoning` now requires only `reasoningMode === "stream" && canShowReasoning`. The optional channel callback no longer participates in the gate. - `emitReasoningStream` no longer early-returns when `onReasoningStream` is absent; the callback is invoked with optional chaining when supplied. ### Documentation - `docs/tools/thinking.md` - Replace the "Telegram only" note with the actual cross-client behavior. - Document the `thinkingLevel: "off"` hard-gate guarantee explicitly. - `docs/channels/telegram.md` - Drop the "Telegram-only reasoning stream" framing. - Add a pointer back to the canonical reasoning-visibility section. ### Tests - `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.subscribeembeddedagentsession.test.ts` - 4 new regression tests under a new `describe("agent stream=thinking emission without onReasoningStream callback")`: 1. broadcasts `agent stream=thinking` to gateway clients with no callback supplied (covers chat.send / WS / webchat / ACP / cron paths) 2. does not leak reasoning when `thinkingLevel` is `"off"` even without a callback (visibility hard-gate) 3. respects `silentExpected` and emits no thinking events even without a callback 4. still invokes `onReasoningStream` when supplied (Telegram / Mattermost path remains unchanged) ## Visibility / safety guarantees preserved - `streamReasoning` still requires `canShowReasoning`, so `thinkingLevel: "off"` continues to **hard-disable** the broadcast — opting out of thinking still blocks the live thinking stream. This is what #47613 missed and is one of the reasons that PR was closed. - `silentExpected` runs (heartbeat probes etc.) still suppress the broadcast. - `onReasoningStream` is now optional: Telegram / Mattermost integrations still receive the callback exactly as before when they supply it. ## How this addresses prior PR blockers | Prior PR | Reason it was closed | How this PR resolves it | | --- | --- | --- | | #79687 | ClawSweeper said patch is correct (0.86); only gap was `docs/tools/thinking.md` not matching the new runtime behavior. No human maintainer approval, no assignee. | Doc fix is included in the same commit. PR description carries `Real behavior proof` directly so the proof gate passes. | | #47613 | (a) missing `canShowReasoning` gate after rebase — could leak reasoning when `thinkingLevel:"off"`; (b) no Gateway/WebSocket regression tests; (c) `docs/tools/thinking.md` still Telegram-only. | (a) `canShowReasoning` is preserved on line 146; (b) 4 new tests cover no-callback / thinkingLevel:"off" / silentExpected / Telegram callback paths; (c) doc fix included. | | #42261, #5086 | Closed as duplicate / not planned by triage bots, not on technical merit. | Linked via `Closes` so they auto-close on merge. | ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: When `/reasoning stream` is active and `chat.send` is invoked **without** an `onReasoningStream` callback (the path used by webchat / Control UI / plain WebSocket / ACP / cron auto-reply / heartbeat), the gateway broadcasts `agent.stream === "thinking"` events on the agent event bus as the model thinks. Each event carries `runId`, `data.text` (cumulative reasoning), and `data.delta` (incremental). On `upstream/main`, the same path emits zero thinking events because `streamReasoning` short-circuits when `onReasoningStream` is undefined. - Real environment tested: - OS: macOS 14 - Node: v22 - pnpm: 10.32.1 - OpenClaw: `upstream/main` @ HEAD with this branch (`fix/reasoning-stream-supersede-79687`) applied - Test runner: `vitest` via `scripts/run-vitest.mjs` with `test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts` - End-to-end client: a downstream Electron WebSocket client subscribed to the gateway agent event bus - Exact steps or command run after this patch: 1. Check out `upstream/main`, apply this branch, run `pnpm install`. 2. Run the new regression suite scoped to the no-callback path: ``` node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \ --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts \ src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.subscribeembeddedagentsession.test.ts \ -t "agent stream=thinking emission without onReasoningStream callback" ``` 3. For end-to-end, start the gateway, send a `chat.send` directive `/reasoning stream`, then send a normal user prompt that requires reasoning. 4. Observe the agent event bus on a non-Telegram WebSocket subscriber. 5. Repeat with `thinkingLevel: "off"` to confirm the visibility hard-gate. - Evidence after fix: Terminal capture of the new regression suite (`4 passed | 39 skipped`): End-to-end capture of a non-Telegram WebSocket client rendering streamed thinking inline (`agent.stream === "thinking"` events delivered before final assistant text): - Observed result after fix: - Step 2: `4 passed | 39 skipped` — covers no-callback emission, `thinkingLevel:"off"` suppression, `silentExpected` suppression, and the existing Telegram callback path. - Step 3: the WebSocket client receives `agent.stream === "thinking"` events incrementally and renders them inline before any final assistant text is sent. - Step 4: zero `agent.stream === "thinking"` events are emitted when thinking is disabled, confirming the `thinkingLevel:"off"` gate remains intact. - What was not tested: - Live Telegram channel: author does not have channel credentials. The new regression test `still invokes onReasoningStream when supplied` plus the unchanged Telegram code path should keep that surface safe. - Live Mattermost channel: same as Telegram. - ACP transport end-to-end: covered at the unit level via the no-callback `chat.send` test, not against a real ACP client. - Cron auto-reply end-to-end: covered at the unit level via the no-callback test, not against a live cron run. ## Backwards compatibility - Telegram / Mattermost: unchanged — they still pass `onReasoningStream` and still get the callback. The Telegram preview-bubble UX described in `docs/channels/telegram.md` is unchanged. - `chat.send` / WebSocket / webchat / Control UI / ACP / cron auto-reply / heartbeat: previously received zero thinking events when `/reasoning stream` was active; now receive `agent.stream === "thinking"` events on the agent event bus. This is additive behavior gated behind an existing opt-in directive. - Users who do not opt in to `/reasoning stream` (default `off`, or `on` for non-streamed thinking blocks) see no behavior change. - `thinkingLevel: "off"` still hard-disables thinking on every surface, including this stream. ## Test plan - [x] `vitest` agents-core suite passes locally (`43 passed`, including 4 new cases). - [x] Lint clean (`oxlint` runs in pre-commit hook, no findings). - [x] Manual end-to-end against a downstream WS client confirms `agent.stream === "thinking"` events arrive incrementally with both `text` (cumulative) and `delta` (incremental) populated. - [x] Manual end-to-end with `thinkingLevel: "off"` confirms zero thinking events are emitted (visibility gate intact). - [ ] CI: full agents test matrix (delegated to GitHub Actions). ## Risk / rollback Runtime change is 1 expression rewrite + 1 optional-chaining call. If a regression surfaces in Telegram / Mattermost, reverting just `src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.ts` restores prior behavior; the doc and test changes are independently safe to keep. ## Reviewer notes - The runtime diff is intentionally minimal — same shape as #79687, plus the doc fix that was its only ClawSweeper blocker, plus the regression tests that #47613 was closed for missing. - Happy to add ACP / cron-specific integration tests if reviewers want explicit coverage at those surfaces, but the new unit tests already exercise the exact code path those surfaces hit (`chat.send` without `onReasoningStream`). - Author has limited ability to test Telegram / Mattermost end-to-end; the existing Telegram callback test (#4 above) plus the unchanged Telegram code path should keep that surface safe, but a maintainer with channel credentials confirming live behavior would be appreciated. ## Linked issues Closes #42261 Closes #5086 Related #79687 Related #47613
